questions.q: The prime number theorem is the statement that, as $x \to \infty$,
questions.hint: Primes thin out logarithmically: roughly one in $\log x$ integers near $x$ is prime.
questions.explain: PNT says $\pi(x)/(x/\log x) \to 1$. Equivalently $\pi(x) \sim \mathrm{Li}(x)$, which is a numerically tighter approximation.

questions.q: Which of $\pi(x)$, $x/\log x$, $\mathrm{Li}(x)$ is the closest numerical approximation to $\pi(x)$ for moderate $x$ (say $x = 10^9$)?
questions.hint: $\mathrm{Li}(x) = \int_2^x dt/\log t$ matches the harmonic-density heuristic better than $x/\log x$.
questions.explain: $\mathrm{Li}(x)$ has the same leading term as $x/\log x$ but absorbs the correction $x/(\log x)^2 + \cdots$, so it tracks $\pi(x)$ much more tightly.
There are $25$ primes $\le 100$. The PNT estimate $100/\log 100 \approx 21.7$, while $\mathrm{Li}(100) \approx 30.1$ — both are in the right ballpark.

questions.q: Which of these directly links the non-trivial zeros of $\zeta(s)$ to the prime-counting error?
choices: The von Mangoldt explicit formula $\psi(x) = x - \sum_\rho x^\rho/\rho - \log 2\pi - \tfrac12\log(1-x^{-2})$
questions.hint: The explicit formula sums $x^\rho/\rho$ over zeros $\rho$.
questions.explain: Apply Perron's formula to $-\zeta'/\zeta$: shifting the contour past the non-trivial zeros picks up a residue $-x^\rho/\rho$ at each, and the trivial pole at $s=1$ contributes $+x$.

questions.q: The Riemann hypothesis ($\mathrm{Re}\,\rho = 1/2$ for every non-trivial zero $\rho$) is equivalent to which sharpening of PNT?
questions.hint: Each zero $\rho$ contributes a term $x^\rho/\rho$; if all $\rho$ have real part $1/2$ the total is $O(x^{1/2}\cdot \#\{\text{zeros up to height }T\})$.
questions.explain: RH is equivalent to $\psi(x) - x \ll x^{1/2}(\log x)^2$. This is the sharpest possible error, because zeros on the critical line force at least $x^{1/2}$ oscillation.

questions.q: The unique pole of $\zeta(s)$ in $\mathbb{C}$ is simple and located at $s = ?$
questions.hint: It is the value of $s$ at which the harmonic series $\sum 1/n$ would diverge.
questions.explain: $\zeta(s)$ has a simple pole with residue $1$ at $s = 1$; the explicit formula's leading term $+x$ in $\psi(x)$ comes from this pole.

questions.q: Hadamard and de la Vallée Poussin's proof of PNT (1896) hinges on which property of $\zeta(s)$?
choices: $\zeta(s)$ has no zeros on the line $\mathrm{Re}\,s = 1$
$\zeta(s)$ has only finitely many zeros in the critical strip
questions.hint: Pushing a zero off the line $\mathrm{Re}\,s = 1$ is the analytic content of PNT.
questions.explain: The classical proof uses the inequality $3 + 4\cos\theta + \cos 2\theta \ge 0$ applied to $|\zeta(\sigma)^3 \zeta(\sigma + it)^4 \zeta(\sigma + 2it)|$ to rule out $\zeta(1+it) = 0$.

questions.q: The classical zero-free region pushes $\zeta$'s zeros into
choices: $\sigma > 1$ (no zeros to the right of the line)
questions.hint: It is a thin sliver to the left of $\sigma = 1$ that narrows as $|t| \to \infty$.
questions.explain: The de la Vallée Poussin region is $\sigma > 1 - c/\log|t|$, narrowing logarithmically; combined with the explicit formula it gives PNT with error $O(x e^{-c\sqrt{\log x}})$.

questions.q: Improving the zero-free region from $\sigma > 1 - c/\log|t|$ to $\sigma > 1/2$ would imply
choices: Dirichlet's theorem on primes in AP
the Riemann hypothesis (and the optimal PNT remainder)
questions.hint: The zero-free region's right edge is the location of the rightmost zero.
questions.explain: Pushing all non-trivial zeros to $\sigma \le 1/2$ is RH; via the explicit formula this yields $\psi(x) - x = O(x^{1/2}(\log x)^2)$.

ant-dirichlet-theorem.title: Dirichlet's theorem on primes in AP

questions.q: Dirichlet's theorem (1837) states that for $\gcd(a, q) = 1$, the progression $a, a+q, a+2q, \ldots$ contains
choices: primes only when $q$ is itself prime
questions.hint: It is a qualitative, not asymptotic, statement.
questions.explain: Dirichlet showed there are infinitely many primes $\equiv a \pmod q$ whenever $\gcd(a, q) = 1$. The quantitative refinement $\pi(x; q, a) \sim \frac{1}{\varphi(q)} x/\log x$ is PNT for APs.

questions.q: The crucial analytic input in Dirichlet's proof is
questions.hint: If $L(1, \chi) = 0$ for some $\chi$, the prime sums over the progression would not diverge.
questions.explain: Orthogonality $\frac{1}{\varphi(q)} \sum_\chi \bar\chi(a) \chi(p) = \mathbf 1_{p \equiv a}$ converts $\sum_{p \equiv a} p^{-s}$ into a sum over $\log L(s, \chi)$. Each non-trivial $L(s, \chi)$ stays finite at $s = 1$ provided $L(1, \chi) \ne 0$, and the trivial character contributes the $\log \zeta(s)$ blow-up.

questions.q: How many residue classes $a \bmod 12$ with $\gcd(a, 12) = 1$ are there? (This is $\varphi(12)$, the number of primes-in-AP classes mod 12.)
questions.explain: $\varphi(12) = \varphi(4)\varphi(3) = 2 \cdot 2 = 4$. Each class $1, 5, 7, 11 \pmod{12}$ contains $\sim x/(4 \log x)$ primes up to $x$.

questions.q: Naive Möbius-weighted inclusion–exclusion $\sum_{d \mid P(z)} \mu(d) \lfloor x/d \rfloor$ counts integers up to $x$ with no prime factor $\le z$. Why does it fail to count primes directly?
choices: The Möbius function is undefined on primes
The error term grows like $2^{\pi(z)}$ when $z$ approaches $\sqrt{x}$, swamping the main term
It only counts prime powers, not primes
It always gives a negative answer
questions.hint: The sum has $2^{\pi(z)}$ terms, each contributing an $O(1)$ error.
questions.explain: Each $\lfloor x/d \rfloor$ has error $O(1)$, and there are $2^{\pi(z)}$ divisors of $P(z) = \prod_{p \le z} p$. Truncating the sum at small $d$ (Brun, Selberg) is the modern fix.

questions.q: Brun's sieve famously gives which conditional twin-prime result?
choices: There are infinitely many twin primes
There are no twin primes above $10^{18}$
questions.hint: Convergence of the reciprocal sum is the celebrated 1919 Brun bound.
questions.explain: Brun (1919) proved $\#\{p \le x : p, p+2 \text{ both prime}\} \ll x/(\log x)^2$, which makes the reciprocal sum converge to Brun's constant $B_2 \approx 1.902\ldots$ — strikingly, this stops short of proving infinitude either way.

questions.q: The Selberg upper-bound sieve finds the optimal weights $\lambda_d$ in
questions.hint: Squares are non-negative, so the LHS is an automatic upper bound for any indicator-truncation of the divisor sum.
questions.explain: Selberg's $\Lambda^2$ trick: $\bigl(\sum_{d \mid n} \lambda_d\bigr)^2 \ge 1$ whenever $\lambda_1 = 1$, so the squared sum is an upper bound. Optimizing $\lambda$ subject to $\lambda_d = 0$ for $d > z$ becomes a quadratic program with a clean closed form.

questions.q: The Bombieri–Vinogradov theorem (1965) bounds $\sum_{q \le Q} \max_{\gcd(a,q)=1} |\pi(x;q,a) - \mathrm{Li}(x)/\varphi(q)|$ for which range of $Q$?
questions.hint: The barrier is $Q \le \sqrt{x}$, the same threshold GRH would yield individually.
questions.explain: Bombieri–Vinogradov works up to $Q \le x^{1/2}/(\log x)^A$ and gives a bound $\ll x/(\log x)^B$. This is GRH-quality on average — pushing $Q$ past $x^{1/2}$ is the Elliott–Halberstam conjecture.

questions.q: Why is Bombieri–Vinogradov as strong as GRH for many applications?
choices: Most arithmetic applications only need an average bound over moduli, not a pointwise bound for each $q$
It eliminates the need for sieve weights
questions.hint: Sieve and prime-distribution arguments typically sum errors over $q$, so $\ell^1$-on-average is the relevant norm.
questions.explain: Many sieve applications (e.g. Chen's theorem, twin almost-primes, bounded gaps) need $\sum_q E(x; q, a)$ small, not each individual $E(x; q, a)$. Bombieri–Vinogradov supplies exactly that, hence the slogan 'GRH on average'.

questions.q: The Elliott–Halberstam conjecture extends Bombieri–Vinogradov by allowing
choices: moduli that are prime powers only
questions.hint: It pushes the cutoff from $\sqrt{x}$ to nearly $x$.
questions.explain: Elliott–Halberstam (1968) conjectures the same average bound holds for $Q \le x^{1-\varepsilon}$. Conditional on it, sieve methods give bounded prime gaps as small as $12$ (Maynard); Zhang's unconditional gap of $70 \cdot 10^6$ used a weakened EH result.
